1: בָּאתִי לְגַנִּי (bati leganni) — 'I have come to my garden' — the immediate fulfillment of the beloved's invitation at the end of Song of Solomon 4. The Qal perfect marks completed action. The fivefold 'I gathered / I ate / I drank / I ate / I drank' follows the chiastic structure of invitation and response. The final summons — 'eat, friends; drink and be intoxicated, lovers (dodim)' — addresses a wider audience: the friends are invited to share the joy of the union.
2: אֲנִי יְשֵׁנָה וְלִבִּי עֵר (ani yeshenah velibbi er) — 'I slept but my heart was awake' — the paradox of the dream-state or half-waking. Libbi er (my heart is awake/watching) uses the same root as the sentinel who guards (ir = watchman, city). The heart that watches while the body sleeps is the deepest level of longing — consciousness at rest but the inner person alert. דּוֹפֵק (dofek) — the participle 'knocking' describes continuous, repeated knocking, not a single knock.
3: פָּשַׁטְתִּי אֶת כֻּתָּנְתִּי (pashatti et kuttaneti) — 'I have taken off my robe' — the excuse uses the same logic as the foot-washing hesitation: the domestic ritual of removing and putting on is used to express the cost and awkwardness of re-dressing. Kuttonet (robe, inner garment) is the same word used for Joseph's robe (Genesis 37:3) and the priestly garments (Exodus 28:4). The beloved's reluctance is tenderly comic; the lover does not force entry but calls and waits.
4: שָׁלַח יָדוֹ מִן הַחֹר (shalach yado min hachor) — 'he thrust his hand through the latch opening' — chor (hole, opening) is the slot in the door for the bolt. The beloved's insertion of his hand through the lock-opening is the most intimate physical action of seeking entry. מֵעַי הָמוּ עָלָיו (me'ai hamu alav) — 'my inmost being stirred for him' — me'ai (bowels, inward parts) is the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology (Genesis 43:30; Isaiah 63:15). The MKT's 'deep longing' captures the visceral nature.
5: מֹר עֹבֵר (mor over) — 'flowing/passing myrrh' — the over (flowing, overflowing) participle describes myrrh that drips spontaneously rather than deliberately applied. Her hands and fingers drip with myrrh that the beloved has left on the door handle — his perfume has transferred to the latch he touched, then to her hands when she opens the door. The fragrance trail is the only trace of his recent presence.
6: נַפְשִׁי יָצְאָה בְדַבְּרוֹ (nafshi yatza'ah bedabbero) — 'my soul went out at his speaking' — the idiom yatza nefesh (the soul goes out) is used in Genesis 35:18 for Rachel's death (her soul going out). Here it captures the near-fainting intensity of the beloved's response to his voice at the moment she has just missed him. בִּקַּשְׁתִּיהוּ וְלֹא מְצָאתִיהוּ (biqqashtihu velo metzatihu) — 'I sought him but did not find him' — the searching of Song of Solomon 3:1-4 is repeated but without the finding.
7: מְצָאֻנִי הַשֹּׁמְרִים (metzauni hashomrim) — 'the watchmen found me' — contrast with 3:3 where they were encountered and questioned; here the watchmen's action is violent. הִכּוּנִי פְצָעוּנִי (hikkuni fetzauni) — 'they beat me, they wounded me' — the violence of the watchmen in this encounter has generated much discussion; the beloved's search in the night streets provokes a defensive reaction from those charged with maintaining order. נְשָׂאוּ אֶת רְדִידִי (nasau et redidi) — 'they took my veil' — stripping the veil was a public shaming gesture (Numbers 5:18; Isaiah 47:2-3).
8: The third and final instance of the adjuration formula to the daughters of Jerusalem, but now with a new content: if they find the beloved, tell him she is חוֹלַת אַהֲבָה (cholat ahavah) — 'sick/faint with love.' This phrase brackets the central section: it appeared first in Song of Solomon 2:5 (she was faint with love while in the beloved's arms); now she is faint while separated from him. The same condition, different circumstances, connects the two scenes.
9: The daughters of Jerusalem ask the theological question of the wasf: what makes your beloved different from all others? מַה דּוֹדֵךְ מִדּוֹד (mah dodekh midad) — 'what is your beloved more than another beloved?' — the comparative uses the same dod root: what makes your dod better than anyone else's dod? The question sets up the extended praise of the beloved in vv10-16 — the most elaborate description of a man's beauty in the Hebrew Bible.
10: צַח וְאָדוֹם (tzach ve'adom) — 'radiant and ruddy' — tzach (dazzling, glowing, pure white) paired with adom (red, ruddy) creates the color portrait of the beloved: both shining and warm-toned. David is described as admoni (ruddy) in 1 Samuel 16:12. דָּגוּל מֵרְבָבָה (dagul meirevavah) — 'outstanding among ten thousand' — degel (banner/standard) reappears in a verbal form: he is 'bannered above ten thousand' — raised like a standard above a multitude. The MKT's 'outstanding' captures the military imagery.
11: קְוֻצּוֹתָיו תַּלְתַּלִּים (qevutzotav taltallim) — 'his hair is wavy/hanging in waves' — taltallim is a rare word, possibly from the root for hanging/dropping; the MKT's 'wavy' suggests the undulating quality. שְׁחֹרוֹת כָּעוֹרֵב (shechorot ka'orev) — 'dark as a raven' — the raven (orev) is jet black; its blackness is the standard for the darkest dark in Hebrew idiom.
12: עֵינָיו כְּיוֹנִים (einav keyonim) — 'his eyes are like doves' — the dove-eye comparison is now applied to the beloved (previously in 1:15 and 4:1 it described the woman's eyes). The dove eyes beside streams of water (afikei mayim) — bathed in milk (rechatzot bechalav), perfectly set (yoshevot al millat) — describe clarity, brightness, and perfect positioning. The eyes that are 'sitting on fullness' (mil'at = fullness/setting) suggests eyes that are complete and satisfied.
13: לְחָיָיו כַּעֲרוּגַת הַבֹּשֶׂם (lechayav ka'arugat habosem) — 'his cheeks are like a bed of spices' — arugah (garden bed, planted section) is a technical agricultural term for a raised planting bed for herbs. The cheeks are fragrant garden plots. שִׂפְתוֹתָיו שׁוֹשַׁנִּים (siftotav shoshanim) — 'his lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh' — the lilies and flowing myrrh combine the floral and the aromatic; his speech is like lilies dipped in myrrh.
14: יָדָיו גְּלִילֵי זָהָב (yadav gelilei zahav) — 'his hands are cylinders/rollers of gold' — gelil (cylinder, roller) is the same word used for the rolled curtain-rods of the Tabernacle (Exodus 26:29). The hands are like the golden rods that hold the sacred curtains — both functional and ornamental. בְּטֶן עַשֶּׁת שֵׁן (beten asheth shen) — 'his torso is polished ivory' — shen (ivory/tooth) was the luxury material for palace furnishings (1 Kings 22:39; Amos 6:4).
15: שׁוֹקָיו עַמּוּדֵי שֵׁשׁ (shoqav ammudei shesh) — 'his legs are columns of alabaster/marble' — shesh (alabaster, fine marble, or Egyptian linen of the same name) creates pillars of smooth, white stone. מֻיָּסָדִים עַל אַדְנֵי פָז (meyusadim al adnei faz) — 'set on sockets of finest gold' — adnim (sockets/bases) is the same technical term used for the silver sockets of the Tabernacle (Exodus 26:19-25). The beloved's legs as Tabernacle pillars on golden bases places the praise of the beloved in the vocabulary of sacred architecture.
16: חִכּוֹ מַמְתַּקִּים (chikko mamtaqim) — 'his mouth is sweetnesses' — the plural mamtaqim expresses the superlative or the multiple kinds of sweetness; his mouth is all sweetnesses. וְכֻלּוֹ מַחֲמַדִּים (vekullo machamadim) — 'and he is entirely desirable' — machamad (desirable, coveted thing) is the same word used for the desirable things of the Temple (Lamentations 1:10; Isaiah 64:11). The beloved is entirely a machamad — the most desirable thing imaginable. The final identification: zeh dodi vezeh re'i (this is my beloved and this is my friend) — the beloved is not only the object of erotic love but a re'ah, a companion and friend.
